title: Strain-Dependent Ionic Transport in Li3YCl6 Solid Electrolytes
authors: Wei-Fan Huang, Jin Dai, Jiahui Pan, Mingjian Wen
abstract: Solid-state batteries require electrolytes that sustain high ionic conductivity under the mechanical environment of a functioning cell. Lattice strain, arising from stack pressure, thermal cycling, or lattice mismatch at interfaces, can either enhance or suppress Li+ transport in solid electrolytes, yet how it couples to the underlying diffusion mechanism remains poorly understood. Using Li3YCl6 halide superionic conductor, we address this with large-scale molecular dynamics simulations driven by an Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE) machine learning interatomic potential trained on first-principles data. The ACE model faithfully reproduces experimental and \textit{ab initio} structural, mechanical, and transport properties of Li3YCl6. We find that Li+ diffusion in Li3YCl6 follows a two-regime Arrhenius behavior, crossing over at a critical temperature $T_c$ from one-dimensional hopping at low temperature to three-dimensional cooperative diffusion at high temperature. Strain substantially modulates diffusivity: tensile strain enhances it while compressive strain suppresses it, yet leaves $T_c$ invariant, indicating that strain tunes diffusion efficiency without reshaping the underlying transport framework. In each regime, the mechanistic origin differs: altered activation barriers dominate at low temperature, while modified pre-exponential factors become critical at high temperature. These results establish lattice strain as a design lever for ionic conductivity in Li3YCl6 solid-state electrolytes.
summary: This paper uses machine learning interatomic potentials to show that mechanical strain can be used as a lever to tune lithium-ion conductivity in halide electrolytes. It reveals that while strain does not change the fundamental diffusion mechanism, it significantly alters the efficiency of ion transport by modifying activation barriers and pre-exponential factors.
main_problem: Understanding how lattice strain (tensile and compressive) affects Li+ ionic transport mechanisms and conductivity in Li3YCl6 solid electrolytes.
main_result: Strain modulates diffusion efficiency without changing the fundamental transport framework or critical temperature (Tc); tensile strain enhances conductivity by increasing the pre-exponential factor, while compressive strain suppresses it.
method: Large-scale molecular dynamics simulations driven by an Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE) machine learning interatomic potential trained via an active learning workflow on DFT data.
model_or_system: The physical system is the Li3YCl6 halide superionic conductor in both trigonal (P-3m1) and orthorhombic (Pnma) phases. The model uses an ACE machine learning potential trained with the optB88-vdW functional to capture van der Waals interactions.
key_observables: Li+ diffusivity (D), ionic conductivity (sigma), activation energy (Ea), pre-exponential factor (D0), critical temperature (Tc), and Li+ probability density maps.
important_parameters: Temperature regimes (low-T 1D hopping vs. high-T 3D cooperative diffusion), strain magnitude (up to +/- 2%), and critical temperature (Tc = 425 K for P-3m1, 380 K for Pnma).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the ACE potential accurately reproduces the underlying DFT energy surface and that isotropic strain is a sufficient proxy for complex mechanical environments.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows crystal structures; Figure 2 shows the active learning workflow; Figure 3 shows model performance metrics; Figure 8 shows Arrhenius plots of diffusivity; Supplemental figures show clustering processes and Li+ probability density maps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of strain in solid electrolytes, describes the development and validation of the ACE machine learning potential, analyzes the two-regime diffusion mechanism, investigates the effects of tensile and compressive strain, and concludes with practical implications for battery design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structures of Li3YCl6. Cl, Li, and Y atoms are shown in orange, green, and blue, respectively.

FIG. 2. Active learning workflow to train ACE models for Li3YCl6. With an initial dataset, the workflow iteratively improves model quality by adding new data for training. The loop stops when the extrapolation grade γ for all molecular dynamics snapshots is smaller than a threshold of 2.

FIG. 3. Performance of the developed ACE model. Histogram of the maximum extrapolation grade γmax in the active learning loop: (a) first model and (b) final model. ACE predicted versus DFT reference (c) energy and (d) atomic forces on the test set obtained using the final ACE model.

FIG. 4. Independent components of the elastic tensor of Li3YCl6. The trigonal P¯3m1 phase and orthorhombic Pnma phase have six and nine independent components, re- spectively. Only the upper triangular part of the elastic tensor is shown, and the lower triangular part can be obtained by symmetry, i.e., Cij = Cji.

FIG. 5. Arrhenius plot of the Li+ diffusivity D. Filled symbols represent results from this work, using ACE trained on DFT data with different functionals (optB88-vdW and PBEsol). Open symbols represent literature data. Experi- mental values: a [25], b [55], c [56], d [48] and e [57]; AIMD simulations (PBE a [48] and PBE b [47]); classical MD simu- lations using MLIPs (MLIP a, fitted to PBE DFT data) [29] and (MLIP b, fitted to optB88-vdW DFT data) [29]. Error bars represent standard deviations from 3 independent MD runs.
